title: Practice Set JA6 — Reading Real Aggregation Code
description: Recognition track capstone: four production-style PySpark pipelines, each 30–50 lines. The code runs, the output looks reasonable, and downstream consumers are happy — but each pipeline carries the bugs and inefficiencies surfaced in JA1–JA5. Read carefully, count shuffles, track null behavior across joins, and answer 4–5 targeted questions per pipeline. The closing task (JA6.4) weaves four bugs together into one dashboard feed.

datasets.description: NordGrid meter readings — 20 rows, the shared sample for the recognition track. Used as the conceptual reference; JA6 pipelines describe production behavior at 500K+ scale.
Substation reference table (6 rows). Joins to meters on substation_id. Every substation_id present in meters has a matching row.
Per-customer-type tariffs (4 rows). Joins on customer_type. Includes one tariff (PROSUMER) absent from meters — surfaces a missing-customer-type scenario.

description.context: You are reviewing a colleague's pull request for the daily regional consumption pipeline. The pipeline reads raw meter CSVs from Bronze, joins to a substation reference table, aggregates daily totals per region, and writes partitioned Parquet to Silver. The code runs and produces correct output. The colleague is asking for a code review before merging.
description.task: Read the full pipeline carefully — line numbers are shown — then answer all four questions below.

evidence.label: daily_regional_pipeline.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, to_date, when, lit

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("daily_regional").getOrCreate()

# Bronze: read raw meter readings
meters = (spark.read
    .option("header", True)
    .option("inferSchema", True)
    .csv("bronze/meters/2026-03-*/"))

# Cast date string to proper DateType
meters = meters.withColumn("date", to_date(col("date"), "yyyy-MM-dd"))

# Drop obvious garbage (sensor errors flag negative readings)
meters = meters.filter(col("daily_kw").isNotNull() & (col("daily_kw") >= 0))

# Substation reference table (Silver layer, already cleansed)
substations = spark.read.parquet("silver/substations/")

# Enrich meter readings with substation metadata
enriched = meters.join(
    substations.select("substation_id", "name", "capacity_mw"),
    on="substation_id",
    how="left"
)

# Aggregate to daily totals per region
daily_totals = (enriched
    .groupBy("region", "date")
    .agg(sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw"))
    .withColumn("level_flag",
        when(col("total_kw") > 1000000, lit("HIGH"))
        .otherwise(lit("NORMAL"))))

# Write to Silver
daily_totals.write.mode("overwrite").partitionBy("date").parquet("silver/daily_regional/")

fields.label: Q1 — Which line triggers the first shuffle of meter data across executors?
fields.rationale: The first shuffle is the join on line 23. CSV reads on line 11 are narrow file scans — no shuffle. The withColumn on line 14 and the filter on line 17 are narrow transformations. The join requires shuffling both meters and substations by substation_id so matching keys land on the same executor — that is the first wide transformation. The groupBy on line 31 triggers a second shuffle (by region and date), but the question asks about the first.

fields.label: Q2 — What happens to a meter row whose substation_id has no match in the substations table?
options: Dropped from the output (silent data loss)
Survives the join with null metadata; daily_kw still aggregates
Raises NullPointerException at the join site
Routed to a Spark-default null-quarantine path
fields.correctAnswer: Survives the join with null metadata; daily_kw still aggregates
fields.rationale: how='left' on line 25 keeps every left-side (meter) row. If substation_id has no match, the join populates name and capacity_mw with null but keeps daily_kw and region intact. The aggregation on line 31 groups by region and date — neither nullable for surviving rows. The orphan-substation reading still contributes to the regional total. This is generally desirable for a consumption report — readings are real even when metadata is incomplete.

fields.label: Q3 — Could the .withColumn on line 14 (date cast) and the .filter on line 17 (drop negatives) swap places without changing the output?
options: Yes — both are narrow, on disjoint columns
No — the filter must come first for performance
Yes — but only if 'date' is already DateType
fields.correctAnswer: Yes — both are narrow, on disjoint columns
fields.rationale: Yes — the cast and the filter operate on independent columns and both are narrow transformations. Catalyst would likely reorder them anyway based on cost. A junior engineer should internalize the principle: narrow transformations on disjoint columns commute. The visible code order does not affect correctness when no transformation depends on another's output.

fields.label: Q4 — Which line is most likely to draw a comment from a junior reviewer who has read PS6?
fields.rationale: Line 17 survives review on most teams but should not. Filtering out null daily_kw is reasonable for a consumption total — but doing it silently, with no separate quarantine path or null count metric, is the same pattern JA4.4 covered. A meter that consistently reports null daily_kw is either decommissioned or has a sensor problem; dropping the row without surfacing it loses both signals. Production code should write null rows to a 'quarantine/null_daily_kw/' path with date and meter_id, so the data quality team can investigate.

description.context: You are reviewing the monthly billing pipeline. It aggregates per (meter_id, month) to compute each meter's bill, joins to a tariff lookup, and produces a per-region monthly summary for the finance dashboard. The pipeline runs end-to-end without errors.
description.task: Before approving the PR, work through the five questions below about the snippet.

evidence.label: monthly_billing.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, sum, count, avg, when, lit, month, year, coalesce
)

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("monthly_billing").getOrCreate()

# Read meter readings (Silver, already cleansed)
meters = spark.read.parquet("silver/meters/")

# Read tariff reference table
tariffs = spark.read.parquet("silver/tariffs/")

# Aggregate per (meter_id, month) — the per-meter monthly bill basis
monthly_per_meter = (meters
    .withColumn("year", year(col("date")))
    .withColumn("month", month(col("date")))
    .groupBy("meter_id", "customer_type", "year", "month")
    .agg(
        sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw"),
        count("*").alias("days_reported"),
        avg("daily_kw").alias("avg_daily_kw")
    ))

# Look up the customer's tariff and compute the bill
billed = monthly_per_meter.join(tariffs, on="customer_type", how="left")

# Defensive: if total_kw is null (meter reported no readings), bill is zero
billed = billed.withColumn(
    "bill_eur",
    coalesce(col("total_kw"), lit(0)) * coalesce(col("eur_per_kw"), lit(0))
)

# Per-region monthly summary for the finance dashboard
# (joins back to meter table to get region — meter_id is the key)
region_lookup = meters.select("meter_id", "region").distinct()
billed_with_region = billed.join(region_lookup, on="meter_id", how="left")

monthly_regional = (billed_with_region
    .groupBy("region", "year", "month")
    .agg(
        sum("bill_eur").alias("total_billed_eur"),
        count("*").alias("bills_issued")
    ))

monthly_regional.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("gold/monthly_regional_billing/")

fields.label: Q1 — How many shuffles does this pipeline trigger?
fields.rationale: Five shuffles total. Wide transformations: groupBy on line 18 (shuffles by meter_id, customer_type, year, month), join on line 26 (shuffles by customer_type — though if tariffs is small enough Spark may broadcast and skip), distinct on line 36 (shuffles to deduplicate), join on line 37 (shuffles by meter_id), groupBy on line 41 (shuffles by region, year, month). This is a heavy pipeline, and the shuffle count itself is a code-review concern.

fields.label: Q2 — What does count('*') on line 21 actually count?
options: Total bills issued (one per (meter_id, year, month))
Days the meter reported a reading in that month
fields.correctAnswer: Days the meter reported a reading in that month
fields.rationale: The input to the groupBy is the meters table — one row per daily reading. After grouping by (meter_id, customer_type, year, month), count('*') counts rows in each group: that is the number of daily readings the meter produced in that (year, month). The alias 'days_reported' is correctly named because each input row is one day's reading per meter. If the source had multiple rows per meter per day (e.g., hourly), this would not be days but hours.

fields.label: Q3 — If a meter_id exists in the meters table but has zero readings in a given month, does it appear in the monthly_per_meter output for that month?
options: Yes — empty groups still produce a row
No — groupBy yields no row for empty groups
Yes — but only with how='outer' on the tariff join
Only if the tariff join is changed to 'right' join
fields.correctAnswer: No — groupBy yields no row for empty groups
fields.rationale: groupBy produces rows only for groups present in the input. A meter that reported zero readings in a month produces zero rows in the input for that month, so no (meter_id, year, month) group exists, and no output row is created. To produce a 'zero bill' row, the engineer would need a complete (meter_id × month) cross-join scaffold first, then left-join the actual readings. This is a common billing-system pattern, absent here — the pipeline silently omits zero-reading meters from the bill.

fields.label: Q4 — What is the role of the coalesce calls on line 31, and what happens if you remove them?
options: They prevent division by zero in the multiplication
They null-guard total_kw and eur_per_kw before billing
They are redundant — Spark already handles nulls
They convert string nulls to numeric zeros for Power BI
fields.correctAnswer: They null-guard total_kw and eur_per_kw before billing
fields.rationale: The coalesce calls protect against null propagation through the multiplication. total_kw could in principle be null if every daily_kw in a group is null (sum() returns null only in that case). More commonly, eur_per_kw is null when a customer_type appears in billing data but not in the tariff lookup — e.g., a new customer_type added upstream while tariffs were not updated. Without coalesce, the multiplication produces null; the per-region sum on line 43 ignores nulls, so the regional total is just the bills that succeeded. With coalesce, missing-tariff bills become zero — same effect but explicit. The engineer should also have surfaced missing-tariff rows as a metric.

fields.label: Q5 — Which line shows a pattern that should make a senior reviewer pause?
fields.rationale: Lines 36-37 do extra work. The meters DataFrame already has a region column, so the cleaner approach is to add region to the groupBy on line 18 — that is, group by (meter_id, customer_type, region, year, month). Region is essentially a function of meter_id (each meter belongs to one region), so adding it does not change row count, but carries region through the aggregation for free. The pattern — aggregate, then re-join to source for a column already available — is the 'lost column join', a common junior-level inefficiency. The fix saves one distinct shuffle and one join shuffle. (Same family as JA6.3.)

tasks.title: The Slow Pipeline That Looks Right

description.context: A teammate's regional summary pipeline produces correct output for the Power BI finance dashboard. The dashboard team is happy with the values. But the pipeline runs in 8 minutes, and a similar pipeline in another team's repo runs in 2 minutes against the same data. Your team lead asks you to identify specifically what is causing the 4× slowdown.
description.task: Read the snippet carefully and answer the four questions below.

evidence.label: regional_summary.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, avg, count, when

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("regional_summary").getOrCreate()

meters = spark.read.parquet("silver/meters/")

# Step 1: per-region totals
regional_totals = (meters
    .groupBy("region")
    .agg(sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw")))

# Step 2: per-region averages (separate aggregation pass)
regional_avgs = (meters
    .groupBy("region")
    .agg(avg("daily_kw").alias("avg_kw")))

# Sanity check that nothing got lost
print(f"Total rows in totals: {regional_totals.count()}")
print(f"Total rows in avgs:   {regional_avgs.count()}")

# Combine the two
summary = regional_totals.join(regional_avgs, on="region", how="inner")

# Step 3: enrich with customer-type breakdown (used by the dashboard slicer)
by_cust_type = (meters
    .groupBy("region", "customer_type")
    .agg(sum("daily_kw").alias("ct_total_kw")))

summary_full = summary.join(by_cust_type, on="region", how="inner")

# Drop INDUSTRIAL — finance excluded from this report
summary_full = summary_full.filter(col("customer_type") != "INDUSTRIAL")

# Final write
summary_full.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("gold/regional_summary/")

print(f"Final row count: {summary_full.count()}")

fields.label: Q1 — Which lines are responsible for the runtime increase?
fields.rationale: Three patterns combine for the 4× slowdown. (1) Separate sum and avg aggregations: two shuffles where one would do, plus a join to reunite them. (2) Intermediate .count() calls: each executes the plan from scratch, re-reading the meters table and re-running the relevant groupBy. (3) Filter applied after the customer_type breakdown: INDUSTRIAL rows participate in the shuffle and aggregation, then get discarded. Pushing the filter before line 28's groupBy would shrink the input by ~25%.

fields.label: Q2 — Of the inefficiencies identified, which one would a junior engineer most likely keep on a first pass at refactoring?
options: All three are equally likely to be kept
fields.rationale: The intermediate .count() prints look like helpful debug output. They are the single hardest pattern to refactor away — partly because the engineer who wrote them genuinely wanted to verify row counts match, and partly because the cost is invisible (a junior reads them as 'just printing a number'). The separate-aggregation pattern is more visible after PS6 lesson 4. The filter-after-groupBy is the one a junior with PS6 lesson 10 freshly read should spot. The intermediate .count() pattern requires understanding lazy evaluation deeply enough to see that .count() is not free — it is a full re-execution.

fields.label: Q3 — What is the production cost of leaving the intermediate .count() calls (lines 19-20) in the pipeline?
options: The .count() calls force a Spark UI refresh
The print() statements log to stdout each call
fields.rationale: Each .count() is an action. Spark does not cache intermediate DataFrames automatically — when you call regional_totals.count(), Spark executes the plan from the meters read forward, computes the sum aggregate, counts the resulting rows. Then on line 20, regional_avgs.count() does it again from the meters read forward, computes avg, counts. Then on line 38, summary_full.count() executes the entire pipeline from scratch. The meters table is read four times (lines 6, 19, 20, 38) — once would be enough if the pipeline were a single plan ending in one write. Most common production-cost mistake at junior level: treating .count() as a free debug tool.

fields.label: Q4 — If you could only make one fix to this pipeline, which would have the largest runtime impact?
options: Combine the sum and avg into one .agg() call
Move the filter on line 35 before the customer_type join
Cache the meters DataFrame after reading it
fields.rationale: Removing the intermediate .count() prints is the largest single win — eliminates two extra full passes over the meters table plus the work that built each DataFrame. The other fixes are real improvements but smaller in isolation: combining sum and avg saves one shuffle; pushing the filter saves ~25% of the customer_type aggregation; caching is a band-aid that does not address the underlying redundancy. Production rule: in development notebooks, .count() is fine. In production code, it is a performance bug. Trigger one action at the end (the write) and verify counts by reading output back if needed.

tasks.title: The Pipeline With the Subtle Bug

description.context: Closing exercise. The pipeline below produces the daily feed for NordGrid's operations dashboard. The output is delivered every morning at 7am and consumed by the operations team for real-time grid management. The pipeline runs cleanly. The dashboard renders. The operations team is using the data. But four distinct bugs from JA1–JA5 are present, and each has a real production consequence.
description.task: Identify all four bugs. Take your time — this exercise rewards careful reading.

evidence.label: ops_dashboard_feed.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum, count, avg, when

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("ops_dashboard_feed").getOrCreate()

# Read the cleansed meter readings (Silver)
meters = spark.read.parquet("silver/meters/")

# Defensive: drop any row with null region (added after the dashboard
# crashed on a null value in February)
meters = meters.filter(col("region").isNotNull())

# Daily-region-customer_type aggregation for the operations dashboard.
# Spec: dashboard shows daily total kW, daily reading count, and daily avg
# per region per customer_type per date.
ops_feed = (meters
    .groupBy("region", "customer_type", "date")
    .agg(
        sum("daily_kw"),
        count("*").alias("readings"),
        avg("daily_kw").alias("avg_kw")
    ))

# Compute a "high-volume flag" for groups whose total exceeds 5000 kW
# (the dashboard highlights these in red)
ops_feed = ops_feed.withColumn(
    "high_volume",
    when(col("daily_kw") > 5000, "YES").otherwise("NO")
)

# Filter to only the most recent month for the dashboard refresh
# (the dashboard shows a 30-day rolling window)
ops_feed = ops_feed.filter(col("date") >= "2026-03-01")

# Sanity-check row count
print(f"Daily-region-cust_type rows: {ops_feed.count()}")

# Roll up to monthly level for the trend panel
trend = (ops_feed
    .groupBy("region", "customer_type")
    .agg(
        sum("readings").alias("month_readings"),
        avg("avg_kw").alias("month_avg_kw")
    ))

# Write both feeds
ops_feed.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("gold/ops_feed_daily/")
trend.write.mode("overwrite").parquet("gold/ops_feed_trend/")

fields.label: Q1 — Bug #1 (null handling). Which line silently drops data, and what is the production consequence?
fields.rationale: The defensive filter on line 11 silently drops every row whose region is null. ~240 meters have null region tags due to upstream tagging issues. Their daily_kw — real consumption from real customers — is dropped before the dashboard sees it. The dashboard under-reports total kW by exactly that amount. Fix: handle null-region rows explicitly (quarantine path, 'UNKNOWN' label, or surface a count metric) — never just filter and forget.

fields.label: Q2 — Bug #2 (aliasing). Which line has a missing alias, and what breaks downstream?
fields.rationale: Line 19 omits .alias('total_kw'). After the .agg(), the column is named 'sum(daily_kw)' rather than 'daily_kw'. The withColumn on line 28 references col('daily_kw') — which no longer exists. PySpark raises AnalysisException at this point. If a try/except were in scope (as in JA3.4), the error would be swallowed and high_volume would be wrong on every row. Fix: .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'), ...) and col('total_kw') in withColumn.

fields.label: Q3 — Bug #3 (count semantics). Which line uses count() in a way that does not match its alias?
options: Line 21 — count('*') ignores the alias's intent
Line 42 — distinct count would be right here
Line 21 is correct; null daily_kw should count
fields.correctAnswer: Line 21 — count('*') ignores the alias's intent
fields.rationale: Line 21 uses count('*') aliased 'readings'. count('*') counts all rows in each group regardless of whether daily_kw is null. If a meter reports a row with null daily_kw (sensor problem, outage, ingestion glitch), it is counted as a reading even though no measurement was produced. Dashboard's reading count is inflated by the null-daily_kw row count. Fix: count('daily_kw').alias('readings') counts only rows where daily_kw is non-null — rows that actually produced a measurement.

fields.label: Q4 — Bug #4 (granularity / aggregation of an aggregate). Which line aggregates a derived metric in a way that produces a misleading number?
fields.rationale: Line 44 computes avg('avg_kw') — the average of daily averages. Mathematically different from the average of all daily readings. Each daily group contributes one avg_kw value to the rollup, and avg() weights every day equally regardless of how many readings the day represented. If one day has 1000 readings averaging 200 kW and another has 10 readings averaging 5000 kW, simple average = 2600 but true average over readings = (1000×200 + 10×5000) / 1010 ≈ 248. 'Average of averages' ≠ 'average of underlying values' unless every group has the same row count. Fix: recompute the monthly average from raw daily_kw values, not by averaging the daily averages.

fields.label: Q5 — Of the four bugs, which one is most likely to remain undetected in production for the longest?
fields.rationale: Bug #4 is the most pernicious. The dashboard renders without errors. The trend panel shows a smooth line. Nobody verifies the math because nobody recomputes from source. The operations team sees the trend, notices that 'average monthly consumption' moves up or down, and acts on it. Values are wrong by an amount depending on day-to-day variation in reading counts — sometimes a few percent, sometimes much more — and the wrongness is invisible to anyone not running their own reconciliation. Bug #1 eventually surfaces through regulatory reconciliation; Bug #2 breaks loudly enough to notice on day one; Bug #3 typically produces a small error that matters in some contexts (billing) but not others. Bug #4 is the one that lives quietly for years.
